notes.note: Rhodo is otherwise unknown; the name suggests a Greek or freedman connected to Thermus's household or provincial staff in Asia whom Cicero had assisted in some unrecorded way.
A Greek contact shared between Cicero and Thermus who had visited Cicero and carried his advice back to the propraetor; identity not further recoverable from the surviving correspondence.

notes.anchor: a young man, powerful and of noble family, were to be slighted by you
notes.note: Cicero is warning Thermus not to demote or humiliate the unnamed quaestor assigned to him, who outranks Thermus's legates by virtue of his elected office; the quaestor's three brothers are said to be due tribune of the plebs positions in successive years, giving him potent political backing in Rome.

notes.anchor: tribunes of the plebs in succession over the next three years
notes.note: The tribunate of the plebs, an annually elected office carrying sacrosanctity and the power of veto (intercessio), was the premier instrument of political harassment; three brothers holding it in sequence could make three years of life in Rome very difficult for an enemy.

notes.anchor: you will avoid enmities and consult for the peace of your later years
notes.note: Cicero's practical advice throughout is that the political climate ahead (already ominous in 50 BC as the Caesar–Pompey crisis deepened) would amplify any personal enemies a man made in his provincial command; picking a needless fight with a nobilis quaestor was simply bad risk management.
